message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-10 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. REFUTED if false, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, on-box data-sovereignty, documented honest-scope), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if it doesn't hold. Use python/pytest/gh as needed.

FINDING P10-PII-2 (low, FIXREG9): _hostname_patterns redacts the bare machine hostname with a trailing \b but no leading boundary and as a substring — over-redacts/corrupts transcripts on nodes with a short or common hostname
Location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238
Claim: The machine-hostname rule compiles `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with re.IGNORECASE and NO leading word boundary, so the bare hostname matches as a substring inside longer tokens. Demonstrated: a hostname `host` redacts inside `localhost` (`local[REDACTED]`) and inside `host.docker.internal`; a hostname `[REDACTED]` redacts inside `[REDACTED]-backups` (`[REDACTED]-backups`). On a CI runner or contributor machine whose hostname is a common substring (e.g. `host`, `ubuntu`, `runner`, `build`), legitimate transcript content gets silently mangled. Unlike the operator-name-token rule (which also omits the leading \b but is explicitly bounded by manual corpus verification of all 256 occurrences) and the handle rule (which requires the whole distinctive letter sequence), the hostname rule has neither protection and pulls names from socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil ComputerName (a human-set name that can be a common word).
Why real: Confirmed by executing the exact compiled pattern against sample text (see substring demonstration). The rule runs at module import on every capture/commit (`_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()`, line 251) on whatever node runs the pre-commit hook. On this node the hostname is the distinctive `[REDACTED]` so live exposure is low, but the logic is node-generic and the only test (test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames) uses a deliberately distinctive `buildbox-7`, so the over-redaction path is untested. Severity is low because committed transcripts are intentionally scrubbed corpus, not runtime data, and the current node's hostname is distinctive.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

200	    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``
201	    forms (a machine name is distinctive). Additional internal hosts (e.g. an RDF-namespace host
202	    like ``[REDACTED]``) are supplied via the comma-separated ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS`` env and have
203	    ONLY their ``.local`` form redacted — the bare label (often a project name like ``hc-grc``)
204	    appears in legitimate paths/repo refs and must not be over-redacted.
205	    """
206	    machine: list[str] = []
207	    try:

239	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form

content.text: The pattern at line 238 confirms the claim's text: `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with `re.IGNORECASE`, no leading boundary. Let me verify the substring behavior empirically.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c '
import re
for b in ["host", "[REDACTED]"]:
    pat = re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE)
    for text in ["localhost", "host.docker.internal", "[REDACTED]-backups", "my-[REDACTED]"]:
        print(repr(b), repr(text), "->", repr(pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)))
'

content.text: The substring over-redaction is empirically confirmed exactly as the finding describes. Now let me check the guardrails: the `len(b) < 4` filter, and whether the comparison rules (operator-name, handle) really differ as claimed. Let me check the test and the other patterns.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -n "buildbox-7\|test_scrub_redacts_machine\|hostname" tests/ -r | head -30; echo "---HANDLE/DISPLAY---"; sed -n '150,200p' scripts/capture_transcripts.py

content.content:
tests/test_scripts.py:199:def test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames(monkeypatch):
tests/test_scripts.py:200:    # #P9-PII-1: the production node's hostname and internal *.local hosts are operator/infra PII
tests/test_scripts.py:201:    # that no email/username/handle rule covered. The machine hostname is redacted bare + .local;
tests/test_scripts.py:206:    # force a deterministic machine hostname regardless of the test host
tests/test_scripts.py:207:    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.socket, "gethostname", lambda: "buildbox-7")
tests/test_scripts.py:208:    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.platform, "node", lambda: "buildbox-7")
tests/test_scripts.py:209:    cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()
tests/test_scripts.py:211:        "ran on buildbox-7.local (bare buildbox-7); ns https://hc-proj.local/prov#; "
tests/test_scripts.py:214:    assert "buildbox-7" not in out  # machine hostname redacted (bare + .local)
tests/test_scripts.py:222:    # names it; the machine hostname auto-derives but this does not. The pre-commit hook must set
---HANDLE/DISPLAY---
    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
    """
    handles: list[str] = []
    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
    try:
        import subprocess

        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
        if out.returncode == 0:
            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
                handles.append(m.group(1))
    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
        pass
    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
    expanded: list[str] = []
    for h in handles:
        expanded.append(h)
        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
            expanded.append(h[3:])
    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
    seen: set[str] = set()
    for h in expanded:
        key = h.lower()
        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
        seen.add(key)
        if len(h) >= 8:
            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
        else:
            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
    return pats


def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
    """Redactions for the operator's machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-MO/P9-PII-1).

    The scrubber masked email/username/display-name/handle but had NO rule for the host the node
    runs on, so the production machine's hostname (e.g. ``[REDACTED]`` / ``[REDACTED]``) and
    internal ``*.local`` mDNS/namespace hosts leaked into committed transcripts — operator/infra
    identity. Derive the MACHINE hostname from ``socket.gethostname``/``platform.node`` (and, on
    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``

content.text:
The finding is accurate on every checkable point:

1. **Pattern confirmed** (line 238): `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"`, IGNORECASE, no leading `\b`.
2. **Substring over-redaction confirmed empirically**: `host` → `local[REDACTED]` in `localhost`, `[REDACTED].docker.internal`; `[REDACTED]` → `[REDACTED]-backups` in `[REDACTED]-backups`.
3. **Comparison to other rules is fair**: the handle rule (line ~187) requires the whole distinctive letter sequence with bounded separator class, and has a `len(h) < 4` plus `>= 8` separator-flex gate. The hostname rule has only `len(b) < 4` — no requirement that the match sit at a token boundary.
4. **Test gap confirmed**: the only test (`test_scripts.py:199`) uses the distinctive `buildbox-7`, which has no common-substring collision, so the over-redaction path is untested.
5. **Runs at import** (line 251).

Let me double-check the only protection (`len(b) < 4`) doesn't actually neutralize the common-word cases the finding cites (`host`=4, `ubuntu`=6, `runner`=6, `build`=5) — all pass the gate.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c '
import re
# common CI/contributor hostnames >= 4 chars pass the len gate
for b in ["host","build","runner","ubuntu","node"]:
    pat = re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE)
    # realistic legit transcript tokens
    samples={"host":"localhost", "build":"rebuild the buildchain", "runner":"the test runner ran", "ubuntu":"on ubuntu-latest", "node":"node_modules/.bin"}
    s=samples[b]
    print(f"{b:8} len_ok={len(b)>=4}  {s!r} -> {pat.sub(\"[REDACTED]\", s)!r}")
'

content.text:
All confirmed. The `len(b) < 4` gate does not protect against any of these common hostnames; substring mangling occurs as the finding describes. (`node_modules` is spared only because `_` is a word char so `\b` doesn't match there — but `localhost`, `buildchain`, `ubuntu-latest`, ` runner ` all mangle.)

The finding is a true, real, demonstrable defect. It correctly bounds severity to **low** (committed transcripts are intentionally scrubbed corpus, the current node's hostname `[REDACTED]` is distinctive, and on-box exposure is limited). But the bug itself — missing leading `\b` causing substring over-redaction on common/short hostnames — is genuine, the comparison to the sibling rules is accurate, and the test-gap claim holds. Nothing refutes it.

input.reasoning: Verified directly against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238. The machine-hostname rule compiles `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with re.IGNORECASE and NO leading word boundary, exactly as claimed. Empirically reproduced the substring over-redaction: hostname `host` -> `local[REDACTED]` in `localhost` and `[REDACTED].docker.internal`; hostname `[REDACTED]` -> `[REDACTED]-backups` in `[REDACTED]-backups`. The only guardrail is `len(b) < 4` (line 235), which does NOT block common CI/contributor hostnames: `host`(4), `build`(5), `runner`(6), `ubuntu`(6) all pass and all mangle legitimate tokens (`local[REDACTED]`, `re[REDACTED]`, `[REDACTED]-latest`, etc.). The comparison to sibling rules is accurate: the repo-handle rule (lines ~176-189) requires the WHOLE distinctive letter sequence (>=8 chars with a bounded `[\s._-]*` separator class) so single common tokens like CISO are not redacted, giving it protection the hostname rule lacks. The test claim also holds: the sole test test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames (tests/test_scripts.py:199) monkeypatches a deliberately distinctive `buildbox-7` that has no common-substring collision, so the over-redaction path is untested. The pattern is built at module import (line 251, `_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()`) and runs on every capture/commit on whatever node runs the pre-commit hook, with the name sourced from socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil ComputerName (a human-set, possibly-common value). Every checkable assertion in the finding is confirmed by the actual code and reproduced behavior. Severity-low framing (scrubbed corpus, distinctive `[REDACTED]` on the current node) is appropriate but the defect is real and node-generic; nothing refutes it.

content.text:
Verdict: STANDS. The finding P10-PII-2 is a real, verified defect, accurately scoped as low severity.

All claims confirmed against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238:
- Pattern `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with IGNORECASE and no leading `\b` — confirmed.
- Substring over-redaction reproduced empirically (`host` -> `local[REDACTED]`; `[REDACTED]` -> `[REDACTED]-backups`).
- The sole `len(b) < 4` guard does not protect common hostnames (`host`, `build`, `runner`, `ubuntu` all pass and mangle).
- Sibling handle rule has genuine whole-sequence protection the hostname rule lacks.
- Test gap confirmed: tests/test_scripts.py:199 uses distinctive `buildbox-7`, leaving the over-redaction path untested.
